name: Rust
description: A systems language focused on memory safety and performance without a garbage collector. Beloved for reliability and speed.

lessons.title: Hello World & println!
lessons.content: Rust uses macros (identified by !) for common operations. println! prints formatted output. Every Rust program starts with a main() function. Rust files have the .rs extension and are compiled with rustc.

exercises.question: How do you print output in Rust?
hints: The ! means it is a macro, not a regular function

exercises.question: What placeholder is used for formatting values in println! ?
hints: Curly braces are the placeholder in Rust format strings

lessons.title: Variables & Mutability
lessons.content: In Rust, variables are immutable by default. Use 'let' to declare. Add 'mut' to make a variable mutable. This immutability-first approach prevents many bugs. Constants use 'const' and require a type.

exercises.question: How do you make a variable mutable in Rust?
hints: The keyword mut comes after let

lessons.title: Ownership & Borrowing
lessons.content: Rust's ownership system manages memory without a garbage collector. Each value has one owner. When ownership moves, the original variable is invalid. Borrowing (&) lets you reference a value without taking ownership.

exercises.question: What happens to s1 after 'let s2 = s1;' in Rust?
options: s1 is moved — it is no longer valid
s1 and s2 both point to the same data
exercises.answer: s1 is moved — it is no longer valid
hints: Rust moves ownership; to keep both, use .clone()

lessons.title: Structs & Enums
lessons.content: Structs group related data. Enums list possible variants — Rust enums can also carry data. The powerful Option enum replaces null: Some(value) or None.

exercises.question: What Rust construct is used to match against enum variants?
hints: match is exhaustive — you must handle every variant
